abstract: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) remain two of the most significant retroviral threats to feline health globally, often leading to severe immunosuppression, secondary infections, and reduced life expectancy. This study provides the first comprehensive assessment of FIV and FeLV prevalence specifically among shelter cats in Portugal, a population at high risk due to unknown medical histories and communal living conditions. Blood samples from 326 stray cats were collected during routine intake examinations at an official shelter in northeast Portugal between 2017 and 2023. Using point-of-care immunochromatographic testing, we identified an overall seroprevalence of 15.3% (50/326). When analyzed by specific pathogen, 9.8% of cats were positive for FIV alone, 2.1% for FeLV alone, and 3.4% were co-infected with both viruses. We found that adult cats were significantly more likely to test positive for FIV and co-infections than kittens, though other demographic factors—such as sex, breed, hair length, and municipality—showed no statistical association with infection rates. These results indicate that while FeLV remains present at a stable low level, FIV prevalence in Portuguese shelters is notably elevated compared to other European regions. Our findings underscore a critical need for universal retroviral screening upon shelter admission, more robust public vaccination efforts, and the implementation of vigilant management strategies to mitigate viral transmission among Portugal’s stray and domestic cat populations.
